heroText: Niamey is a capital city in Niger, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Niamey
highlights.text: Niamey is a capital city in Niger, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.label: Origins
history.text: Background and foundation The area of modern Niamey was of little importance before Niger's colonial era.
The first European mentions of Niamey were by the 1897 mission of Émile Auguste Léon Hourst, then the 1899 Voulet–Chanoine Mission, which set fire to Niamey.
Niamey had about 1,800 inhabitants in 1904, when it became the capital of the Third Military Territory of Niger.
The capital moved back to Zinder in 1911, after violence in the eastern region had subsided and reorganisation had drawn Niamey further away from the center of the territory.
As capital, Niamey also became the center for the educated class of mostly Songhai and Zarma people, with the first regional school opening in 1930.
Most residents wished to stay on the river bank, but, after a 1935 fire destroyed many straw buildings, they were forcibly moved to mudbrick houses, resulting in multiethnic neighbourhoods.
Despite this, housing insecurity increased, and the informal settlement of Talladjé was created in 1966.
On 9 April 1999, Baré was ambushed, shot, and killed at Niamey's airport during a coup d'état.
In 2013, American and French military bases were established at adjacent parts of Diori Hamani International Airport.

history.label: Civic role
history.text: The villages of Goudel and Gamkalé were founded on the left bank (east side) of the Niger River in the sixteenth century.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Those who remained established informal settlements with mostly thatched mud buildings on the edge of the indigenous quarter.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Most of the urban plan was cancelled during the Second World War, when colonial officials left for the war and the administration prioritised commercial farming over infrastructure.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Niamey received the country's only asphalt highway, spanning a few kilometres between Diori Hamani International Airport and the Presidential Palace—the former governor's palace.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: This plan redrew municipalities' boundaries based on pre-colonial settlements, with consulting from traditional chiefs.
